summary: Gruber examines WebKit's new text-wrap: pretty CSS feature, now available in Safari Technology Preview 216, which uses a multiline algorithm to improve text wrapping on the web. He's turned it on at Daring Fireball as an experiment but has mixed feelings. While individual paragraphs look better with more uniform line widths, the algorithm creates jarring width disparities between consecutive paragraphs, which he finds distracting. He suspects the algorithm works better with wider column widths and suggests it needs tweaking for narrow columns like those on phones. Despite his reservations, he praises the WebKit team for tackling a problem that has left web typography crude for over 30 years.
key_points: WebKit's text-wrap: pretty implements a multiline wrapping algorithm similar to InDesign and LaTeX, replacing the web's 30-year-old single-line approach
Individual paragraphs look better with prettier rag, but paragraph-to-paragraph width consistency suffers, creating a distracting visual effect
The algorithm appears to work better with wider column widths, suggesting Daring Fireball's current narrow layout exposes its weaknesses
Narrow columns on phones present an inherent challenge for the algorithm that Gruber thinks needs addressing
Despite mixed results, Gruber welcomes the WebKit team tackling web typography's long-standing crudeness
money_quotes: Line-to-line the new algorithm looks better, but paragraph-to-paragraph I think it looks worse.
But you don't take paragraphs on their own when reading.
Look at me, fast adopter of novel CSS features.
There's so much disparity between paragraph widths that it's distracting, even though each paragraph, taken on its own, looks better.
As Simmons notes, line-wrapping layout in web browsers has, until now, been very crude — and the web has been around a long time.
I couldn't be happier that the WebKit team is even tackling the problem at all.
key_insight: WebKit's new multiline text-wrapping algorithm improves individual paragraph typography but introduces a new problem of inconsistent paragraph widths that becomes distracting in actual reading flow.
